# Codebook: `gp_analysis_dataset.csv`

**Location:** `2. Data/3. Processed/gp_analysis_dataset.csv`  
**Unit of observation:** Gram Panchayat (GP)  
**Sample:** 14-state Kjelsrud sample, restricted to GPs with non-missing treatment (`fragmentation_2004_past`), `pc_dist` FE cell, all nine controls, and at least one non-missing outcome  
**Rows:** 47,995 GPs  
**Columns:** 33  
**Generated by:** `3. Scripts/GP_level_analysis.ipynb`, Section 15  
**Used by:** `3. Scripts/GP_level_analysis.do` (Stata replication)

---

## How to reproduce regressions from this file alone

```python
# Python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("gp_analysis_dataset.csv")

# Main regressions use: fragmentation_std, ln_*_targeting, controls (list below), pc_dist
# ST regression additionally filters: df[df['in_st_sample'] == 1]
# Quadratic adds: fragmentation_std_sq
```

```stata
* Stata
import delimited using "gp_analysis_dataset.csv", clear
* See GP_level_analysis.do for full regression code
```

---

## Variable Groups

### 1. Identifiers

| Variable | Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| `gp_id` | int | Kjelsrud internal GP identifier (1–151,660). Primary key. | `nrega.dta` |
| `state` | float | Census 2011 state code (numeric). See state code map below. | `nrega.dta` |
| `state_name` | string | Human-readable state name corresponding to `state` code. | Derived |
| `district2011` | int | Census 2011 district code (national numbering). | `nrega.dta` / `gp_id_names.dta` |
| `pc_id_pre` | float | Pre-2008-delimitation parliamentary constituency ID. | `nrega.dta` |
| `pc_id_post` | float | Post-2008-delimitation parliamentary constituency ID. | `nrega.dta` |
| `pc_dist` | float | **Fixed effects cell.** Pre-delimitation PC × district interaction (1–1,260 unique values). Use as the absorbed FE in all regressions. | `nrega.dta` |
| `change_pc` | binary | = 1 if GP was reassigned to a new parliamentary constituency in the 2008 Delimitation Act. 24.4% of GPs. | `nrega.dta` |

**State code map:**

| Code | State | Code | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | Punjab | 22 | Chhattisgarh |
| 6 | Haryana | 23 | Madhya Pradesh |
| 9 | Uttar Pradesh | 24 | Gujarat |
| 10 | Bihar | 27 | Maharashtra |
| 19 | West Bengal | 28 | Andhra Pradesh |
| 21 | Odisha | 29 | Karnataka |
| — | — | 32 | Kerala |
| — | — | 33 | Tamil Nadu |

---

### 2. Treatment Variable

| Variable | Type | N | Mean | SD | Min | Median | Max | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `fragmentation_2004_past` | float | 47,995 | 0.642 | 0.079 | 0.438 | 0.644 | 0.810 | Political competition of the **post-delimitation** PC. Defined as 1 − HHI of candidate vote shares in the April–May 2004 general election. Higher = more competitive. Source: Kjelsrud replication package (`nrega.dta`). |
| `fragmentation_std` | float | 47,995 | −0.214 | 0.932 | −2.612 | −0.191 | 1.757 | **Main regressor.** `fragmentation_2004_past` standardised to zero mean, unit SD across the full 14-state sample. Coefficient interpretable as effect of a 1-SD increase in political competition. |
| `fragmentation_std_sq` | float | 47,995 | 0.913 | 0.942 | 0.000 | 0.590 | 6.822 | `fragmentation_std²`. Used as the quadratic term in the Section 11c non-linear specification. |

> **Why 2004?** The 2004 election pre-dates the Delimitation Commission's work (which started July 2004), so it could not have been influenced by anticipation of boundary changes. Kjelsrud demonstrates competition is persistent across electoral cycles.

---

### 3. Outcome Variables

#### 3a. Regression outcomes (winsorised log form — use these in regressions)

| Variable | Type | N | Mean | SD | Min | Median | Max | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `ln_sc_targeting` | float | 44,915 | −0.018 | 1.088 | −7.612 | 0.048 | 3.055 | Log SC targeting ratio (winsorised at 1st–99th pct before log-transform). **Outcome for SC regression.** Missing for GPs not matched to both Mittal person-day and population data. |
| `ln_st_targeting` | float | 28,005 | −0.057 | 1.315 | −7.526 | −0.025 | 4.090 | Log ST targeting ratio (winsorised). **Outcome for ST regression.** Use with `in_st_sample == 1` filter. |
| `ln_scst_targeting` | float | 47,995 | −0.040 | 0.894 | −7.742 | 0.023 | 2.100 | Log SC+ST targeting ratio (winsorised). **Outcome for SC+ST regression.** |

**Targeting ratio definition:**

```
TargetingRatio(G, j) = [person-days by group G in GP j / total person-days in GP j]
                     / [population of G in GP j  / total rural population in GP j]
```

Ratio = 1 → proportional representation. > 1 → over-represented. < 1 → under-represented.  
Winsorised at the 1st and 99th percentiles of the non-missing distribution before log-transforming. `log(0) = −∞` set to NaN.

#### 3b. Raw ratios (for descriptives and Figure 1 — do NOT use as regression outcomes)

| Variable | Type | N | Mean | SD | Median | Max | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sc_targeting_ratio` | float | 46,496 | 2.800 | 32.752 | 1.020 | 4,397.7 | Raw (not winsorised). Extreme right skew — max reflects GPs with near-zero SC population share. |
| `st_targeting_ratio` | float | 33,451 | 4.006 | 39.422 | 0.866 | 2,687.3 | Raw. Includes GPs with very low ST population (denominator noise). |
| `scst_targeting_ratio` | float | 47,995 | 1.628 | 10.277 | 1.023 | 872.9 | Raw. |

---

### 4. Population Shares and Sample Flags

| Variable | Type | N | Mean/Pct | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `sc_pop_share` | float | 47,995 | mean=0.174 | SC population / total rural population in GP (from `panchayat_category.csv`). Enters targeting ratio denominator. **Do not use as a regression control** — it is already in the denominator of the outcome. |
| `st_pop_share` | float | 47,995 | mean=0.166 | ST population / total rural population. Median 0.014 reflects many GPs with near-zero ST population. Used to define `in_st_sample`. |
| `in_st_sample` | binary | 47,995 | 38.7% = 1 | = 1 if `st_pop_share ≥ 0.05` AND `ln_st_targeting` non-missing. **Apply `if in_st_sample == 1` for all ST regressions.** Excludes GPs where near-zero ST denominator makes the ratio uninformative. |
| `has_sc_outcome` | binary | 47,995 | 93.6% = 1 | = 1 if `ln_sc_targeting` is non-missing. Reflects successful name-match to both Mittal datasets. |
| `has_st_outcome` | binary | 47,995 | 58.3% = 1 | = 1 if `ln_st_targeting` is non-missing (before the ≥ 5% ST pop filter). |

---

### 5. Control Variables

All controls come from the Kjelsrud replication package (`nrega.dta`), itself derived from the Census of India 2001. They are time-invariant across the 2011–2013 panel years in `nrega.dta`. **All nine must be non-missing for a GP to be in the base sample** (enforced in data preparation).

| Variable | N | Mean | SD | Min | Median | Max | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `share_l6_past` | 47,995 | 0.163 | 0.029 | 0.101 | 0.161 | 0.227 | Share of village population aged under 6 (Census 2001). Proxy for household labour supply and MGNREGA demand. |
| `share_lit_past` | 47,995 | 0.543 | 0.097 | 0.239 | 0.554 | 0.857 | Literacy rate (Census 2001). Higher literacy → greater awareness of MGNREGA entitlements and ability to navigate applications. |
| `poverty_pre66_past` | 47,995 | 0.332 | 0.187 | 0.003 | 0.305 | 0.825 | Poverty headcount (NSS pre-2066 rounds, PC-level estimate). Structural demand for the programme. |
| `ln_population` | 47,995 | 14.442 | 0.167 | 13.707 | 14.430 | 15.325 | Log of rural population (`population_past` from Census 2001). GP size affects absolute and per-capita MGNREGA allocation. |
| `urbanization_past` | 47,995 | 0.222 | 0.136 | 0.000 | 0.196 | 0.983 | Urbanisation rate (Census 2001 PC-level). Programme utilisation differs even within the rural sample. |
| `primary_past` | 47,995 | 0.889 | 0.112 | 0.419 | 0.922 | 1.000 | Share of villages in PC with a primary school (Census 2001). Proxy for baseline state capacity and service delivery. |
| `phc_past` | 47,995 | 0.047 | 0.040 | 0.000 | 0.037 | 0.747 | Share of villages with a primary health centre (Census 2001). Baseline health/admin infrastructure. |
| `paved_past` | 47,995 | 0.644 | 0.255 | 0.192 | 0.719 | 1.000 | Share of villages with a paved road (Census 2001). Physical access to worksites. |
| `power_past` | 47,995 | 0.641 | 0.314 | 0.007 | 0.683 | 1.000 | Share of villages with electricity (Census 2001). Proxy for general infrastructure development. |

> **Note:** `share_sc_past` and `share_st_past` (see Section 6) are intentionally **excluded** from the regression controls. SC/ST population shares are in the denominator of the targeting ratio outcomes. Including them as regressors would mechanically partial out the denominator and bias the coefficient on political competition.

---

### 6. Diagnostic Variables (not used in regressions)

| Variable | N | Mean | SD | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `share_sc_past` | 47,995 | 0.159 | 0.085 | SC population share of the GP (Census 2001, from `nrega.dta`). For balance tables and descriptives only. |
| `share_st_past` | 47,995 | 0.157 | 0.216 | ST population share of the GP (Census 2001, from `nrega.dta`). For balance tables and descriptives only. |

---

## Data Linkage Summary

The dataset merges four source files. The binding constraint on sample size is the `panchayat_category.csv` name-match (~65K GPs matched out of 151K).

```
nrega.dta          →  treatment, FE (pc_dist), controls           [151,660 GP-years → 150,413 GPs]
gp_id_names.dta    →  GP name strings for name-matching           [151,660 GPs]
mis_avg_sc_st_data.csv  →  person-days by caste (numerator)       [~119K GPs matched, ~79%]
panchayat_category.csv  →  SC/ST population counts (denominator)  [~65K GPs matched, ~43%]
```

After requiring non-missing controls, treatment, and at least one outcome: **47,995 GPs** in the base sample.

### Match rate balance check

Matched and unmatched GPs differ slightly on key variables (from notebook Section 9):

| Variable | Unmatched | Matched | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| `fragmentation_2004_past` | 0.672 | 0.639 | −0.033 |
| `change_pc` | 0.281 | 0.246 | −0.035 |
| `share_sc_past` | 0.196 | 0.155 | −0.041 |
| `share_st_past` | 0.095 | 0.149 | +0.054 |

Matched GPs are slightly less politically competitive and have higher ST shares. This should be discussed as a limitation: estimates may not generalise to the ~22% of unmatched GPs, which tend to be in lower-competition, lower-SC constituencies.

---

## Regression Quick-Reference

| Section | Outcome | Sample filter | Regressors | FE | Cluster |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 11 (1) | `ln_sc_targeting` | `has_sc_outcome == 1` | `fragmentation_std` + controls | `pc_dist` | `pc_dist` |
| 11 (2) | `ln_st_targeting` | `in_st_sample == 1` | `fragmentation_std` + controls | `pc_dist` | `pc_dist` |
| 11 (3) | `ln_scst_targeting` | full base sample | `fragmentation_std` + controls | `pc_dist` | `pc_dist` |
| 11c | `ln_st_targeting` | `in_st_sample == 1` | `fragmentation_std` + `fragmentation_std_sq` + controls | `pc_dist` | `pc_dist` |
| 14 (5–7) | all three | same as above | `fragmentation_std` only (no controls) | `pc_dist` | `pc_dist` |

**Controls list** (9 variables): `share_l6_past`, `share_lit_past`, `poverty_pre66_past`, `ln_population`, `urbanization_past`, `primary_past`, `phc_past`, `paved_past`, `power_past`
